You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for an Uber/Amazon-style senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD system prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, with all the prescribed sub-structure, emoji markers (🎬 🎙️ 💬 🗣️ 🧠 🤝 🆘 ⚠️ 🔀 ❓), and the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; IGNORE its old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths — the real root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes, NO §1–§9-LLD structure. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code that ever appears: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in the API section, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers exactly — only the rounded results are surfaced in the markdown, never unrun code. REQUIRED SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, in this order — match the headings): ## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional) — 🎬 structure pitch opener; 3-5 functional flows "above the line" each with feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why it's hard; "Below the line (out of scope)" list; NFRs each with a talkable target (p99 ms, 9s) + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks without it; close with 🎙️ Script. TEXT ONLY. ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions — each: spoken question, 🗣️ why I'm asking, ↔️ design fork (If YES.../If NO...), Assumption I'll proceed with; end with 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — a table: Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE the arithmetic by writing a capacity.py and running it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python capacity.py — paste the rounded results. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + its flip threshold. Plain-text math only (~, x, ÷, no LaTeX). End with a short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — per entity: name + what it owns, 🗣️ simple analogy, ⚠️ interviewer probe + one-line answer; 🎙️ Script. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+ why this verb), 2-4 key request fields (+ why; call out server-set fields), a concrete JSON request+response example, one key design decision (idempotency key etc.); security note (caller from JWT, never trust client ids/prices). 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: one-sentence framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted); 🗣️ key-terms gloss block; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action / why this component / state change / what could go wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing line; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" mermaid flowchart TD that is the reconciled union of all slices + 🎙️ Script + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (Entity | key fields with PK/shard key | chosen store | partition key | consistency); PART B a storage decision card per distinct store (what it is / why this — the access pattern that forces it / considered & rejected / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table. 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating Bad/Good/Great; each tier: ↩️ what previous got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach (mechanism) / ⚠️ what breaks (with concrete numbers) / 🔁 what forces the upgrade; Good & Great get a tier-specific mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision-forcing math + ✅ failure matrix. Each ends with 🎙️ Script + 🧠 "If they ask". Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, failure behavior. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s per path + human downtime + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (Component | what breaks | how it degrades gracefully — what USER SEES | recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define both in plain words first, then table per data class); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + why + rough monthly order-of-magnitude + the one biggest optimization). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards "🔀 Decision: [chose] vs [didn't]": what we chose & why / what we gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses (exact flip condition) / 🗣️ how to say it. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains (core algorithm/hardest part, failure, scale/hot-spot, consistency, security, cost, extensibility), 2-3 each, 12-18 total; each: ❓ question verbatim, the mechanism (2-3 sentences, terms glossed), 🗣️ in plain words, 💬 one-liner to say. End with a 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — get them right; HLD has MANY diagrams so this matters a lot): - Use ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT to "flowchart TD" for architecture/multi-path (NEVER flowchart LR for multi-path — it stretches off screen; LR only for a trivial 3-4 node linear chain). Use sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of these breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces { }. A semicolon ';' or colon ':' or parens inside a label/message BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks inside a node, never a raw newline. - Edge labels: short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| (text inside the pipes must itself avoid the breaking chars above — no ':' or ';' or parens inside it). - Same component = same node id/label across every diagram. Cap ~10-12 nodes per diagram. - Each §6.x slice gets its own diagram; §6 ends with one "Final (high-level)" union diagram; deep-dive Good/Great tiers get mechanism diagrams. Aim for 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . It must be LONG and comprehensive (the md must be > 20,000 chars; realistically 40-55K). Pure markdown — tables with real line breaks, fenced code only for the JSON API examples and mermaid. No LaTeX. Emoji markers as prescribed. - You MAY create a scratch workspace dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ to write capacity.py and run it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Do NOT write into /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles the JSON afterwards. Before finishing: (1) verify capacity.py actually runs and you used its real computed numbers; (2) self-check that all 11 section headings are present and the md is > 20K chars; (3) count your ```mermaid blocks and make sure each one's labels are free of the breaking characters listed above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 84929a55-c466-4e23-af3a-581f842f3fdd TITLE: Design a Flash Sale System PROBLEM BRIEF (this is what transcript[0] will say; frame §1 around it): A flash-sale / limited-inventory system: sell exactly K items with NO overselling under a massive concurrent spike (e.g. 1M users for 1K items). Focus on the inventory decrement race at scale, queueing/admission control, and fairness. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these — this is what makes the session strong): preventing oversell under extreme concurrency (atomic decrement in Redis / reservation tokens), admission control + queue (virtual waiting room), the single-hot-key inventory counter as the binding bottleneck + how to shard/relieve it, idempotent checkout, payment + reservation timeout/release. Deep dives: oversell prevention (the hot inventory counter — the global coordinator), admission control / waiting room, reservation + timeout release, fairness vs throughput. Assume an Amazon/Uber senior-SDE (SDE-3 / L5) bar: cost & operations are first-class, work backwards from the customer, decide and move. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/84929a55-c466-4e23-af3a-581f842f3fdd_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first, write+run capacity.py for the numbers, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars = actual char count, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. For EACH endpoint write: 336 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 337 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 338 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 339 ``` 340 // Request 341 POST /v1/orders 342 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 343 344 // Response 200 345 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 346 ``` 347 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 348 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 349 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 350 351 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 352 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 353 ### 6.x "" 354 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 355 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 356 357 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 358 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 359 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 360 361 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 362 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 363 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 364 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 365 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 366 367 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 368 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 369 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 370 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 371 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 372 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 373 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 374 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 375 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 376 377 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 378 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 379 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 380 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 381 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 382 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 383 384 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 385 386 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 387 388 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 389 390 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 391 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 392 393 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 394 395 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 396 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 397 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 398 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 399 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 400 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 401 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 402 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 403 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 404 405 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 406 407 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 408 409 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 410 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 411 412 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 413 414 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 415 416 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 417 418 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 419 420 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 421 422 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 423 424 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 425 426 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 427 428 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 429 430 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 431 432 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 433 434 Each deep dive ends with: 435 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 436 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 437 438 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 439 440 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 441 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 442 443 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 444 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 445 446 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 447 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 448 449 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 450 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 451 452 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 453 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 454 455 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 456 457 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 458 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 459 460 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 461 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 462 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 463 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 464 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 465 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 466 467 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 468 469 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 470 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 471 472 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 473 474 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 475 476 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 477 478 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 479 480 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 481 482 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 483 484 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 485 486

content.text: Now let me write the capacity.py script to compute the talkable numbers for the flash sale system.

input.content:
"""Capacity math for a Flash Sale System.
Scenario: 1,000,000 concurrent users racing for 1,000 items at sale open (T0).
All numbers are rounded to talkable figures in the markdown; this file shows the derivation.
"""

def r(n):
    """Round to a talkable phrase."""
    if n >= 1_000_000:
        return f"{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
    if n >= 1_000:
        return f"{n/1_000:.0f}K"
    return f"{n:.0f}"

print("=== FLASH SALE CAPACITY ===\n")

# --- Demand at sale open ---
concurrent_users = 1_000_000          # users hammering the page at T0
items_for_sale   = 1_000              # K, the limited inventory
# Assume each waiting user retries / refreshes the "buy" button ~3x in the first second
retries_per_user = 3
spike_window_s   = 1                  # the brutal first second
print("Concurrent users at T0:", r(concurrent_users))
print("Items for sale (K):", items_for_sale)
print("Oversubscription ratio:", f"{concurrent_users/items_for_sale:.0f}:1  (1M buyers chasing 1K units)")

# --- Peak inbound request rate (the buy button storm) ---
# Each user fires the buy intent, then retries. Worst-case all land in the first second.
buy_attempts = concurrent_users * retries_per_user
peak_buy_qps = buy_attempts / spike_window_s
print("\nPeak buy attempts in first second:", r(buy_attempts))
print("Peak buy QPS (no admission control):", r(peak_buy_qps), "=", f"{peak_buy_qps:,.0f}/s")

# --- The binding bottleneck: the single hot inventory counter ---
# One Redis node does atomic DECR at roughly this rate for a single key:
redis_single_key_ops = 100_000        # ~100K ops/s ceiling on ONE hot key on ONE node
print("\n--- HOT KEY BOTTLENECK ---")
print("Single Redis node single-key ceiling:", r(redis_single_key_ops), "ops/s")
print("Peak demand vs ceiling:", f"{peak_buy_qps:,.0f}/s demand  vs  {redis_single_key_ops:,.0f}/s ceiling")
overload = peak_buy_qps / redis_single_key_ops
print("Overload factor:", f"{overload:.0f}x over the single-key ceiling -> MUST relieve the hot key")

# --- Relief 1: admission control / virtual waiting room ---
# Only let through a trickle that the inventory tier can actually serve.
# We only have 1000 items, so we never need more than a few x that many *winning* attempts.
# Admit at a rate the counter shard can absorb comfortably, e.g. 10K/s of REAL buy attempts.
admit_rate = 10_000
print("\n--- ADMISSION CONTROL ---")
print("Admitted buy QPS (waiting room drip):", r(admit_rate), "/s")
print("Reduction from raw spike:", f"{peak_buy_qps/admit_rate:.0f}x  (3M/s -> 10K/s)")
# Time to drain enough admits to sell out:
# inventory sells out almost instantly once admits flow; the room then just shows sold-out.
sellout_attempts_needed = items_for_sale  # 1000 successful decrements
print("Successful decrements needed to sell out:", items_for_sale)
time_to_sellout_s = sellout_attempts_needed / admit_rate
print("Time to sell out at admit rate:", f"{time_to_sellout_s*1000:.0f} ms (essentially instant)")

# --- Relief 2: shard the counter into N stripes ---
# If we ever needed K = 1,000,000 items (not 1,000), one key can't take the write rate.
shards = 10
per_shard_ops = peak_buy_qps / shards
print("\n--- COUNTER SHARDING (only needed for large K) ---")
print("If we split the counter into", shards, "stripes of K/N each:")
print("Per-shard write rate:", r(per_shard_ops), "/s  (still ~", f"{per_shard_ops/redis_single_key_ops:.0f}x over -> sharding alone is NOT enough, need admission too)")
print("Conclusion: admission control is the primary relief; sharding helps only for large K.")

# --- Reservation timeout / release ---
reservation_ttl_s = 120               # 2 minutes to pay
print("\n--- RESERVATION / TIMEOUT ---")
print("Reservation TTL:", reservation_ttl_s, "s (2 min to complete payment)")
# How many reservations can be 'in flight' at once = inventory (1000), each held up to TTL
# Released stock re-enters the pool; with 1000 items the release worker load is tiny.
print("Max in-flight reservations:", items_for_sale, "(bounded by inventory, trivial load)")

# --- Payment tier sizing ---
# Only winners pay. Winners <= K = 1000. Payment is the cheap part.
payment_p99_ms = 800
print("\n--- PAYMENT TIER ---")
print("Only winners (<= K =", items_for_sale, ") ever call payment -> tiny, ~1000 total calls")
print("Payment p99 budget:", payment_p99_ms, "ms (async, outside any lock)")

# --- Read tier: the product/landing page during the storm ---
# 1M users staring at the page, polling status every ~2s
page_poll_interval_s = 2
read_qps = concurrent_users / page_poll_interval_s
print("\n--- READ TIER (page + status polling) ---")
print("Status-poll read QPS:", r(read_qps), "/s =", f"{read_qps:,.0f}/s")
# Cache hit ratio for a single hot product page is ~99.9% (one object, fully cacheable at edge)
cache_hit = 0.999
miss_qps = read_qps * (1 - cache_hit)
print("CDN/edge cache hit ratio:", f"{cache_hit*100:.1f}%")
print("Residual origin read QPS after cache:", r(miss_qps), "/s =", f"{miss_qps:,.0f}/s (origin barely notices)")

# --- p99 budget decomposition for the buy path (admitted user) ---
print("\n--- p99 BUY-PATH BUDGET (admitted request) ---")
budget = {
    "edge + LB":        15,
    "gateway + authz":  20,
    "admission check":  10,
    "redis atomic decr":  5,
    "reservation write": 25,
    "network hops":     25,
}
total = sum(budget.values())
for k, v in budget.items():
    print(f"  {k:<20} {v} ms")
print(f"  {'TOTAL':<20} {total} ms  (target p99 < 200 ms for the decrement, payment async)")

# --- Cost (order of magnitude, monthly) ---
print("\n--- COST (rough monthly, sale is bursty) ---")
# CDN egress dominates: 1M users * ~500KB page * a few page loads
page_kb = 500
loads = 5
cdn_gb = concurrent_users * page_kb * loads / (1024*1024)
print("CDN egress per sale event (GB):", f"{cdn_gb:,.0f} GB")
cdn_cost_per_gb = 0.05
print("CDN egress cost per sale:", f"${cdn_gb*cdn_cost_per_gb:,.0f}")
print("Redis cluster (always-on, modest):", "~$2K-5K/mo")
print("Waiting-room + app autoscale (burst):", "~$3K-8K per big sale")
print("Dominant driver: CDN egress + burst compute during the spike, NOT the DB.")

print("\n=== KEY DECISION NUMBER ===")
print("Demand 3M buy-attempts/s vs single-key ceiling 100K/s = 30x over.")
print("FLIP THRESHOLD: if peak admitted demand < ~80K/s a single Redis key suffices;")
print("above that you MUST add admission control (and shard the counter only if K is large).")

input.content:
# Design a Flash Sale System

> The problem: sell **exactly K items** (e.g. 1,000 sneakers) to a crowd of **1,000,000 concurrent buyers** the instant the sale opens — with **zero overselling**, fair access, and a site that doesn't fall over. The whole game is the inventory-decrement race at extreme concurrency, plus admission control so the spike never reaches the parts that can't take it.

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — the buy path, the waiting room, the reservation lifecycle. After that I'll go deep on the three hardest parts: preventing oversell on the hot inventory counter, admission control, and the reservation-timeout race — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: **1M people want the same 1,000 units in the same second.** Inventory is a single shared number that everyone is trying to decrement at once. If two buyers both read "1 left" and both decrement, we oversold — and overselling a physical item means refunds, angry customers, and lost trust. So the design is really *one hard problem* (a contended counter) wrapped in machinery to keep the stampede away from it.

> 🏢 I'll target an **Amazon SDE-3 bar**: cost and operations are first-class, I work backwards from the customer (a fair shot at the item, a clear "you got it / you didn't"), and I'll decide and move rather than enumerate every option.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — Browse the sale & see live availability.** Users should be able to open the product page before and during the sale and see whether stock remains.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Anyone can pull up the sneaker page and see 'available' or 'sold out', even if that number is a second or two behind reality."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** 1M people refreshing one page is 500K reads/s on a *single* object — the read storm alone can melt an origin if it isn't fully cached at the edge.

**FR2 — Join the sale & be admitted fairly (virtual waiting room).** When demand exceeds what the buy path can serve, users should be placed in a waiting room and admitted in a controlled, fair order.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If too many people show up at once, we put them in a digital line and let them through a few at a time, in roughly the order they arrived — like a bouncer at a club door."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** you must throttle 3M buy-attempts/s down to a trickle the inventory tier can absorb, *without* letting anyone slip the queue, and the queue itself can't become the new bottleneck.

**FR3 — Attempt to buy (atomic reserve, no oversell).** An admitted user should be able to claim one unit; the system either grants a hold or tells them it's sold out — and never grants more than K holds total.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When it's your turn, you tap Buy and instantly find out 'it's yours, pay now' or 'sorry, gone' — and we can never hand out more 'it's yours' than we actually have."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is the binding bottleneck — a single counter being decremented by a torrent of concurrent writers, where a single missed lock = a real oversell.

**FR4 — Check out & pay within a time limit (reservation → order).** A buyer who holds a reservation should complete payment within a short window; if they don't, the unit is released back to the pool.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Once you've got a hold, you have two minutes to pay. Don't pay in time and the shoe goes back on the shelf for someone else."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the hold must be released *exactly once* on timeout, payment must be idempotent (no double-charge on retry), and the released unit must safely re-enter the count without reintroducing an oversell.

**Below the line (out of scope):** product catalog management & merchandising, recommendations/search, post-sale shipping & logistics, returns/refunds workflow, fraud/bot detection beyond basic rate limiting (I'll mention it but not design it), and multi-item carts (flash sale is one SKU, quantity one).

### Non-Functional (talkable targets)

**NFR1 — No overselling, ever (correctness over everything).** Hard invariant: granted holds + completed orders ≤ K, always.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We will never sell the 1,001st unit of a 1,000-unit drop, full stop."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** two buyers pay for the last pair, we cancel one order, eat a refund, and a customer who 'won' now feels cheated — the single worst outcome for this product.

**NFR2 — Survive the spike without falling over (availability ~99.9% during sale, graceful shed above that).** The buy path should answer in **p99 < 200 ms** for admitted requests; the read/page path stays up at 500K reads/s.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Three nines means under ~9 hours of downtime a year, but more importantly: the site doesn't crash when a million people hit it at once — extra load gets a polite waiting-room page, not an error."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the whole spike hits the database, connection pools exhaust in seconds, and *everyone* sees a 500 — including the people who would have bought.

**NFR3 — Fairness (bounded, best-effort FIFO admission).** Admission should be roughly first-come-first-served; no one with a faster bot should consistently beat patient humans.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If you got in line first, you should generally get your shot first — we don't want bots vacuuming the whole drop."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the entire inventory goes to scalpers/bots in milliseconds, real customers never get a chance, and the brand takes the reputational hit.

**NFR4 — Idempotent, exactly-once money.** A given checkout must charge at most once even under client retries, network timeouts, and crashes.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If your phone retries the payment because the network blipped, you still only get charged once."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** retries during the chaos double-charge customers — a trust and compliance disaster.

> 💬 "The whole design lives or dies on one shared number — the inventory count — so I'm going to spend most of my budget protecting that counter and keeping the crowd away from it, not on the boring parts."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So functionally there are four flows: see the item and whether it's available, get admitted through a fair waiting room, atomically claim a unit without overselling, and pay within a timeout or lose the hold. The non-functionals that actually shape the design are: never oversell — that's a hard invariant; stay up under a million concurrent users by shedding load gracefully instead of crashing; be fair so bots don't eat everything; and make money idempotent so retries don't double-charge. Everything hard about this problem is the single contended inventory counter, so that's where I'll go deep."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "Is the inventory truly scarce and fixed at K, or is it effectively unlimited like a digital coupon?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** if stock is unlimited I can throw away the entire reservation-and-decrement machinery; if it's a hard K, that race *is* the problem.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If scarce (K=1,000) → atomic decrement + reservations + waiting room are the core. If unlimited → just rate-limit and log; no counter contention at all.
- **Assumption:** scarce, fixed K (1,000), physical goods. This is the hard, interesting case.

**Q2 — "Roughly how many concurrent users hit at the open — thousands, or a million?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** the spike size decides whether one Redis key survives or whether I *must* add admission control and possibly shard the counter.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If ~50K → a single atomic counter handles it, skip the waiting room. If ~1M → admission control is mandatory (we'll see the math forces it).
- **Assumption:** ~1M concurrent at T0 (the brief). This forces a virtual waiting room.

**Q3 — "Do we own the payment processor, or is it an external gateway like Stripe with its own latency and failure modes?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** an external processor takes hundreds of ms and can fail — so I must keep payment *outside* any lock and make it async, otherwise the lock-hold time kills throughput.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If external → reserve-first, pay-second saga with timeout release. If in-house → I could in theory do a tighter transaction, but I'd still split it for resilience.
- **Assumption:** external processor (~800 ms p99, can fail). Reserve-then-pay saga.

**Q4 — "How strict is fairness — strict global FIFO, or is rough first-come-first-served plus anti-bot good enough?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** strict global ordering across a million clients is extremely expensive (a global sequencer); rough FIFO per shard is cheap and usually what the business actually wants.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If strict → a global token issuer / sequencer (a coordinator, with its own ceiling). If rough → per-region queues with timestamped tokens, far cheaper.
- **Assumption:** rough best-effort FIFO + basic per-account/IP rate limiting. Throughput and no-oversell beat perfect ordering.

**Q5 — "Is one unit per customer the rule, and how long do they get to pay?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** the per-user limit changes dedup keys, and the payment window sets the reservation TTL and how fast unpaid stock recycles.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If 1-per-user → dedup on user_id at reserve time. If N-per-user → quantity logic and a different decrement.
- **Assumption:** 1 unit per customer; 2-minute payment window (reservation TTL = 120s).

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to assume a hard 1,000-unit drop, ~1M concurrent users, an external payment processor, rough FIFO fairness, and one-per-customer with a two-minute pay window. If any of those is off — especially the scale or the fairness strictness — tell me now, because they change the design materially."

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

All arithmetic below was computed by running `capacity.py`; I'm surfacing only the rounded, say-out-loud figures.

> **Glossary, glossed once:** **QPS** = queries (requests) per second. **Atomic decrement** = a single indivisible "subtract one and tell me the result" that no other writer can interleave with. **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a value lives before it auto-expires.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Oversubscription | 1,000,000 users ÷ 1,000 items | 1000:1 | "a thousand buyers chasing every single unit" | Why fairness + admission matter at all |
| Peak buy QPS (raw) | 1M users x ~3 retries ÷ 1s spike | ~3.0M/s | "about three million buy taps in the first second" | The number that forces admission control |
| Single hot-key ceiling | one Redis node, one key ~ 100K ops/s | ~100K/s | "one Redis key tops out near a hundred thousand a second" | The binding bottleneck ceiling |
| Overload factor | 3.0M ÷ 100K | ~30x | "we're thirty times over what one counter can take" | MUST shed load before the counter |
| Admitted buy QPS | throttle the spike to what the tier serves | ~10K/s | "we drip about ten thousand real attempts a second through" | Sizing the admission rate (300x cut) |
| Time to sell out | 1,000 successful decrements ÷ 10K/s | ~100 ms | "the 1,000 units are gone in a tenth of a second" | Waiting room is mostly showing 'sold out' |
| Status-poll reads | 1M users ÷ 2s poll interval | ~500K/s | "half a million status checks a second" | Read tier must be edge-cached |
| Origin reads after cache | 500K x (1 − 99.9% hit) | ~500/s | "after the CDN, the origin sees only ~500/s" | One cache layer makes reads a non-issue |
| Buy-path p99 budget | 15+20+10+5+25+25 ms (sum) | ~100 ms | "an admitted buy clears in about a tenth of a second" | Keeps us under the 200 ms SLO |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** **~3M buy-attempts/s vs a ~100K/s single-key ceiling = 30x over.** A single Redis counter physically cannot absorb the spike.
**Flip threshold:** if peak *admitted* demand stays under **~80K/s**, one Redis key is enough and I'd skip the waiting room entirely. Above that, admission control is mandatory. (Counter *sharding* only helps when **K itself is large** — for K=1,000 the issue is write rate, not the count, so admission is the right lever, not sharding.)

*Storage is a non-issue here* — 1,000 reservations and at most 1,000 orders is a few KB; this problem is about write contention and load shedding, not bytes.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything: about three million buy taps land in the first second, but a single Redis counter tops out near a hundred thousand a second — so we're thirty times over what one key can take. That's why I can't just point a million people at a counter; I have to shed load first with a waiting room, dripping maybe ten thousand real attempts a second through, at which point the thousand units sell out in about a tenth of a second. Reads are easy by comparison — half a million status checks a second, but a CDN absorbs 99.9% so the origin sees only a few hundred. So my whole budget goes on protecting that one counter, not on storage or reads."

---

## 4. Core Entities

> This is a first draft of the nouns; I'll add full field definitions in §7.

**InventoryCounter** — the single source of truth for "units remaining" for a sale. Owns the atomic decrement that decides winners.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a turnstile with a hard cap — it lets exactly K people through and then physically locks."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do two buyers racing for the last unit not both win?" → the decrement is atomic and returns the post-value; only the writer who sees a result ≥ 0 wins, everyone else gets sold-out.

**Reservation** — a temporary hold on one unit for one user, created at the moment of a successful decrement, with a TTL.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a 'hold' tag on the shelf — set aside for you for two minutes, then it goes back if you don't pay."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "What if the user never pays?" → on TTL expiry a release worker returns the unit to the counter — exactly once.

**Order** — the durable record created when payment succeeds; the reservation's terminal, paid state.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "the receipt — proof the sale actually happened and money changed hands."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can one reservation become two orders on retry?" → no; the idempotency key on checkout collapses retries to one order.

**WaitingRoomToken (AdmissionToken)** — a signed pass that says "you're allowed to attempt a buy now," issued by the admission controller in fair order.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a numbered ticket from the deli counter — when your number's called you can step up, not before."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can someone fake a token to skip the line?" → no; tokens are signed and short-lived, validated server-side at the buy endpoint.

**Sale** — the campaign config: which SKU, K, start time, per-user limit, reservation TTL.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "the event poster — what's on sale, how many, when the doors open."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you arm the sale at exactly T0?" → the counter is pre-loaded with K and the gate flips at start_time; before that, all buys are rejected at the edge.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The nouns are: the InventoryCounter — the one shared number everything fights over and the heart of the design; the Reservation, which is a short-lived hold created the instant you win a decrement; the Order, the durable paid record; the AdmissionToken, the signed 'it's your turn' pass from the waiting room; and the Sale config. The one to watch is the InventoryCounter — it's the single coordinator, and almost every hard question traces back to it. I'll flesh out the fields in the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

**Security stance (say this once, applies everywhere):** the caller's identity comes from their **JWT** *(a signed login token the server can verify without a database lookup)* — never from a `userId` in the body. The client never sends price or quantity-it-wants-to-pay; the server reads K, price, and limits from the Sale config. The client cannot forge state (`status`, `reservationId`) — the server sets those.

### FR1 — Read availability → `GET /v1/sales/{saleId}`
- **Why GET:** pure read, fully cacheable at the edge.
- **Key fields:** `saleId` (path). No body.
- **Example:**
```
GET /v1/sales/drop_air_2026
// Response 200 (served from CDN, ~1s stale)
{ "saleId": "drop_air_2026", "status": "LIVE", "remaining": "available", "startsAt": "2026-06-21T17:00:00Z" }
```
- **Key decision:** the response says `"available"` / `"sold_out"`, **not an exact count** — exposing the precise number invites scraping and makes the cache churn on every decrement. Coarse status is cacheable for ~1s.

### FR2 — Join the waiting room → `POST /v1/sales/{saleId}/queue`
- **Why POST:** creates server-side state (your place in line).
- **Key fields:** `saleId` (path); identity from JWT. No client-set position.
- **Example:**
```
POST /v1/sales/drop_air_2026/queue
// Response 202
{ "queueToken": "qt_8f...", "position": 48213, "etaSeconds": 35, "pollAfterMs": 2000 }
```
- **Key decision:** returns `202 Accepted` with a `pollAfterMs` — the client polls a status endpoint; when admitted it receives a short-lived signed `admissionToken`. Position is **server-assigned** from arrival time (rough FIFO), never client-supplied.

### FR3 — Attempt to buy / reserve → `POST /v1/sales/{saleId}/reservations`
- **Why POST:** creates a Reservation (new state).
- **Key fields:** `admissionToken` (proves it's your turn — server-validated, signed); `Idempotency-Key` header (so a retry doesn't double-reserve). Identity from JWT. **No price, no quantity** — server enforces 1-per-user from config.
- **Example:**
```
POST /v1/sales/drop_air_2026/reservations
Headers: Idempotency-Key: 2c1a-...   Authorization: Bearer <jwt>
{ "admissionToken": "at_d4..." }
// Response 201 (you won)
{ "reservationId": "rsv_77a", "status": "HELD", "expiresAt": "2026-06-21T17:02:00Z", "amount": 18900, "currency": "usd" }
// Response 409 (sold out)
{ "error": "SOLD_OUT" }
```
- **Key decision:** the **atomic decrement happens here** and is the only place inventory changes. The `amount` is set by the **server** from the Sale config — the client never names a price. Same `Idempotency-Key` returns the *same* reservation, not a second one.

### FR4 — Pay & confirm → `POST /v1/reservations/{reservationId}/checkout`
- **Why POST:** transitions the reservation to a paid Order.
- **Key fields:** `paymentMethodId` (a token from the processor — we never see the raw card, keeping us out of **PCI scope** *(the rules for handling card data)*); `Idempotency-Key` header.
- **Example:**
```
POST /v1/reservations/rsv_77a/checkout
Headers: Idempotency-Key: 9b3e-...
{ "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123" }
// Response 200
{ "orderId": "ord_55c", "status": "CONFIRMED" }
// Response 410 (hold expired before you paid)
{ "error": "RESERVATION_EXPIRED" }
```
- **Key decision:** payment runs **outside any lock** (the unit is already reserved). The idempotency key makes a retried checkout return the existing order instead of charging twice. If the hold expired, you get `410` and the unit is already back in the pool.

> 💬 "Notice the client never sends a price or a userId or its own queue position — the server owns all of that. The only things the client supplies are signed tokens and an idempotency key."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four endpoints, one per flow. A GET on the sale returns a coarse available-or-sold-out — coarse on purpose, so it caches and doesn't leak the exact count. POSTing to the queue gets you a place in line and, when it's your turn, a signed admission token. POSTing to reservations is where the one atomic decrement lives — that's the whole ballgame — and it returns HELD or SOLD_OUT. Then checkout pays outside any lock with an idempotency key so retries don't double-charge. Throughout, identity is from the JWT and price is from our DB, never the request — the client can't tamper with who they are or what they pay."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)

> **Color palette (used in every diagram):** blue = clients, green = our services, amber = queues/async, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 "Browse the sale & see live availability"

Framing: a million people stare at one page and poll its status — this is a read-fan-out problem solved almost entirely at the edge.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN edge cache | A global network of caches near users that serves the page without touching our servers | One hot object polled 500K/s — the edge absorbs 99.9% so origin sees ~500/s | Not origin-only (500K/s melts it); not per-request DB read (pointless for one object) | Status is ~1s stale (fine — it's coarse anyway) |
| Read service | A small stateless service that renders the coarse status when the edge misses | Owns the available/sold-out logic and TTL | Not reading the live counter per request (that re-creates contention) | Slightly stale status |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a fleet of caches spread worldwide that serve content from near the user. Here it absorbs the 500K/s page-and-status storm so our origin barely notices.
> **Origin** — our actual servers behind the CDN. Here it only sees cache misses, ~500/s.

**Diagram — read path (FR1)**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Buyer app]:::client
    U -->|"1 GET sale status"| CDN[CDN edge cache]:::ext
    CDN -->|"2 miss only"| RS[Read service]:::svc
    RS -->|"3 read coarse status"| KV[(Status cache Redis)]:::store
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2a5c,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: none (first slice).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Buyer requests sale status.** Hits the nearest CDN edge. *Why here:* the edge is the only thing that can take 500K/s. *State:* none changed. *What if:* edge node down → CDN routes to another edge automatically.
2. **On a miss, edge calls the read service.** *Why:* the read service owns the coarse status and TTL. *State:* none. *What if:* read service overloaded → it's stateless, autoscale; worst case serve last cached value.
3. **Read service reads coarse status from Redis.** *Why:* a tiny in-memory read, never the contended counter. *State:* none. *What if:* Redis blip → serve `LIVE` optimistically; the buy path is the real gate, so a stale "available" just sends a few people to a 409.

> 💬 "The trick is we never read the *exact* count on the hot path — we serve a coarse available-or-sold-out that caches for a second, so a million pollers turn into a few hundred origin reads."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "For browsing, a million people polling one page is half a million reads a second — way too much for an origin. So I put it all behind a CDN, which is just a global cache near users; it absorbs about 99.9% and the origin sees a few hundred a second. And I deliberately show a coarse available-or-sold-out instead of the exact count, so the cache stays warm and we don't leak the live number to scrapers."

### 6.2 "Join the sale & be admitted fairly (virtual waiting room)"

Framing: the spike is 30x what the counter can take, so before anyone reaches inventory we put them in a line and admit a controlled rate in arrival order.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Admission controller | A service that hands out a place in line and, at a fixed rate, signed "your turn" tokens | Converts a 3M/s stampede into a steady ~10K/s admitted trickle — a 300x cut | Not letting everyone hit inventory (30x over ceiling); not a single global lock (that IS the bottleneck) | Some users wait; ordering is rough not perfect |
| Queue store | A Redis sorted-set keyed by arrival time, holding positions | Cheap rough-FIFO ordering, in-memory, fast | Not a durable queue like Kafka per-user (overkill — losing a waiting-room slot is recoverable) | Queue is not durable; on crash, re-queue |
| Rate gate | A token bucket that releases admissions at the target rate | Lets us tune admitted QPS to exactly what inventory can absorb | Not unthrottled (re-creates the spike) | Admitted rate is a tuned dial, set per sale |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Virtual waiting room** — a digital line that holds users and admits them gradually. Here it sheds the spike so inventory never sees more than it can handle.
> **Token bucket** — a rate limiter that releases a fixed number of permits per second. Here it sets the admitted buy rate (~10K/s).
> **Sorted set** — a Redis structure that keeps members ordered by a score. Here the score is arrival time, giving rough FIFO.
> **Admission token** — a short-lived signed pass proving it's your turn. Here it's the only way to reach the buy endpoint.

**Diagram — admission path (FR2)**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Buyer app]:::client
    U -->|"1 join queue"| AC[Admission controller]:::svc
    AC -->|"2 add by arrival time"| QS[(Queue store sorted set)]:::store
    AC -->|"3 release at rate"| RG[Rate gate token bucket]:::svc
    RG -->|"4 mint admission token"| U

**Numbered step narration**
1. **User joins the queue.** Admission controller assigns a position by arrival timestamp. *Why:* one place owns fair ordering. *State:* a queue entry created. *What if:* duplicate joins → dedup on user_id, one slot per user.
2. **Entry added to the sorted set by arrival time.** *Why:* O(log n) ordered inserts, in-memory fast. *State:* queue grows. *What if:* Redis blip → re-queue on reconnect; losing a slot just re-times you.
3. **Rate gate releases admissions at the tuned rate.** *Why:* a token bucket caps admitted QPS to what inventory can absorb. *State:* a batch of users marked admitted. *What if:* gate set too high → inventory tier sees pressure; we tune it below the safe ceiling.
4. **Admitted user is minted a signed admission token.** *Why:* the buy endpoint trusts only this token. *State:* user can now attempt a buy. *What if:* token forged → signature check fails at the buy endpoint.

> 💬 "Admission control is the whole reason this works — we turn a three-million-a-second stampede into a ten-thousand-a-second trickle, a 300x cut, before anyone touches the counter."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Because the spike is thirty times what one counter can take, I put a virtual waiting room in front of inventory — basically a bouncer. Everyone joins a Redis sorted set ordered by arrival time, and a token-bucket rate gate releases people at a tuned rate, say ten thousand a second, handing each a signed admission token. That's a 300x reduction, and the buy endpoint only trusts that token, so nobody can skip the line. Fairness is rough, not perfect, but that's the right trade — throughput and no-oversell matter more than strict ordering."

### 6.3 "Attempt to buy (atomic reserve, no oversell)"

Framing: this is the binding bottleneck — the one place inventory changes — and the entire design exists to feed it a safe, throttled stream.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reservation service | Validates the admission token, runs the atomic decrement, writes the hold | Owns the single mutation point for inventory | Not letting clients touch Redis directly (no auth/validation) | Extra hop (~25 ms, within budget) |
| Inventory counter (Redis) | A single in-memory integer decremented atomically by a Lua script | Atomic DECR-and-check is the cleanest no-oversell primitive at this speed | Not a Postgres row with SELECT FOR UPDATE (lock + disk = far slower under spike); not optimistic retries (thundering herd) | In-memory; needs durable mirror for crash recovery |
| Reservation store | Durable record of each hold with a TTL | Survives a Redis crash; source for the timeout-release worker | Not Redis-only (volatile — a crash would lose holds) | One extra write (~25 ms) |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Lua script (in Redis)** — a tiny program Redis runs atomically as one indivisible step. Here it decrements the counter and creates the hold so no other writer can interleave.
> **SELECT FOR UPDATE** — a SQL row lock held until the transaction commits. Considered and rejected here — it serializes on disk and is too slow for the spike.
> **Atomic compare-and-decrement** — "if count > 0, subtract one, else reject," done indivisibly. Here it's the no-oversell guarantee.

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Admitted user attempts to reserve, presenting the token.** Reservation service validates the signature. *Why:* the service is the only authenticated path to inventory. *State:* none yet. *What if:* token expired → 401, user re-queues.
2. **Service runs the atomic decrement (Lua).** *Why:* one indivisible "decrement if >0" makes the race impossible. *State:* counter drops by one *iff* it was positive. *What if:* counter already 0 → script returns sold-out, no write.
3. **Only a non-negative result wins.** *Why:* the decrement returns the post-value; ≥0 means you got a real unit. *State:* winner determined. *What if:* concurrent writers → Redis serializes them; exactly K can win.
4. **Service writes the hold with a TTL to the durable reservation store.** *Why:* survives a Redis crash and feeds the timeout worker. *State:* a HELD reservation exists. *What if:* write fails after decrement → compensating return-to-counter (see §8 failure matrix).
5. **Emit a `reserved` event.** *Why:* downstream (analytics, notifications) consume async. *State:* event appended. *What if:* event log down → buy still succeeds; events replay later.

> 💬 "Two buyers can't both win the last unit because the decrement is one atomic step — Redis serializes them, and only the writer who sees a result of zero or more actually got a shoe."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "This is the heart. The reservation service validates your admission token, then runs a tiny Lua script in Redis that says 'if count is above zero, subtract one and return the new value, else reject' — all in one indivisible step. That single atomicity is the entire no-oversell guarantee: Redis serializes every writer, so exactly a thousand of them can come back non-negative. I rejected a Postgres row lock because locking and disk under a three-million-a-second spike is far too slow. The trade is the counter is in memory, so I also write a durable hold and keep a mirror for crash recovery."

### 6.4 "Check out & pay within a time limit (reservation → order)"

Framing: payment is slow and external, so it runs outside the lock; an unpaid hold must release exactly once on timeout.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Checkout service | Charges via the processor using an idempotency key, then writes the Order | Money must be exactly-once and outside any inventory lock | Not charging inside the decrement (lock held across 800 ms = throughput death) | A reservation can expire before payment (acceptable, releases unit) |
| Payment processor | External gateway (e.g. Stripe) that charges the card | We don't store cards (PCI scope) | Not in-house payments (out of scope, huge build) | ~800 ms latency, can fail — handled by saga |
| Release worker | A scheduled worker that returns expired holds to the counter | Recycles unpaid stock so it isn't lost | Not relying only on Redis TTL (TTL frees the hold but must also re-increment the counter exactly once) | Slight delay (seconds) before stock recycles |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Idempotency key** — a client-supplied unique id so a retried request is recognized and not re-executed. Here it ensures one charge and one order even under retries.
> **Saga** — a sequence of steps with compensating actions instead of one big transaction. Here: reserve → pay → confirm, with "release on timeout" as the compensation.
> **Release worker** — a background job that returns expired holds to inventory exactly once. Here it recycles unpaid stock.

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Buyer submits checkout with an idempotency key.** *Why:* checkout owns the money step. *State:* none yet. *What if:* duplicate retry → same key returns the existing order, no second charge.
2. **Checkout charges the processor with the key.** *Why:* external gateway keeps us out of PCI scope. *State:* charge attempted. *What if:* processor times out → retry with same key; charge is idempotent on their side too.
3. **Charge succeeds.** *Why:* payment is the slow external step, kept outside any lock. *State:* money captured. *What if:* charge fails → reservation stays HELD until TTL, then released.
4. **Checkout writes the Order durably.** *Why:* the order is the terminal paid record. *State:* CONFIRMED order exists. *What if:* crash after charge before write → reconcile by idempotency key (§8).
5–6. **Release worker returns expired, unpaid holds to the counter exactly once.** *Why:* recycle stock without re-oversell. *State:* counter incremented; reservation marked EXPIRED. *What if:* worker runs twice → idempotent release (guarded by reservation status flip) prevents double-increment.

> 💬 "Payment is slow and external, so it never sits inside the lock — the unit is already reserved; we just have a two-minute clock, and if it runs out the release worker puts the shoe back exactly once."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Once you hold a unit, checkout charges the external processor with an idempotency key, so a retry returns your existing order instead of charging twice, then writes a durable confirmed order. Critically, payment runs outside any lock — the unit's already yours for two minutes. If you don't pay in time, a release worker returns the unit to the counter exactly once and the next person can grab it. It's a saga: reserve, pay, confirm, with timeout-release as the compensation."
